incidents.victim: 200+ organizations worldwide (government agencies, law enforcement, email providers)
incidents.vector: Exploited known vulnerabilities in 18,000+ end-of-life SOHO routers (Mikrotik, TP-Link) to hijack DNS settings and intercept Microsoft Office OAuth tokens via adversary-in-the-middle attacks.
incidents.impact: Authentication tokens harvested from 5,000+ devices across 200+ organizations, bypassing MFA and granting direct access to victim accounts without deploying malware.

incidents.victim: Axios NPM package (100M+ weekly downloads)
incidents.vector: Social-engineered the lead Axios developer via a fake company video call to install malware, then compromised the NPM package to inject a malicious dependency (plain-crypto-js) deploying the WAVESHAPER.V2 backdoor.
incidents.impact: Malicious versions were live for approximately three hours, deploying platform-specific RATs on Windows, macOS, and Linux. Credentials on affected systems should be considered compromised.

incidents.victim: European Commission (Europa.eu platform on AWS)
incidents.vector: Exploited the Trivy supply chain compromise (CVE-2026-33634) to steal an AWS API key, then exfiltrated 92 GB of compressed data including 52,000 email-related files from 42 internal clients across 29 EU entities.
incidents.impact: Stolen data appeared on ShinyHunters dark web site on March 28. Names, email addresses, and some email content of EU staff exposed. CERT-EU attributed the breach to TeamPCP with high confidence.

incidents.victim: Multiple organizations (Trivy, Checkmarx KICS, LiteLLM, Telnyx, VS Code plugins, 66+ NPM packages)
incidents.vector: TeamPCP executed a multi-phase supply chain campaign starting with Trivy (Mar 19), spreading via CanisterWorm to NPM/Docker/GitHub Actions, then compromising Checkmarx KICS, LiteLLM, and Telnyx packages to harvest credentials at scale.
incidents.impact: Estimated 300 GB credential trove stolen. Group now operating dual ransomware tracks (CipherForce and Vect). Downstream victims include Databricks (investigating) and AstraZeneca (3 GB data leaked by LAPSUS$). CISA issued KEV entry with April 8 deadline.

incidents.victim: Stryker (medical technology company)
incidents.vector: Wiper attack by Iranian government-connected group Handala, claimed as retaliation for the US-Israel conflict with Iran.
incidents.impact: Order processing, manufacturing, and shipping disrupted for three weeks. Company declared fully operational on April 2. Patient care support was the stated top priority during recovery.

incidents.victim: FBI Director Kash Patel (personal email)
incidents.vector: Iranian group Handala claimed to breach Patel's personal email inbox and posted contents online, including travel details, correspondence, and loyalty account numbers.
incidents.impact: Personal data including Global Entry numbers and travel itineraries published online. FBI stated no government information was taken. State Department offered reward for information on Handala.

incidents.victim: 300+ organizations across critical infrastructure (Israel, UAE, US, UK, Europe, Saudi Arabia)
incidents.vector: Password-spraying campaign against Microsoft 365 environments targeting municipalities, satellite, aviation, energy, and maritime sectors. Targeting correlated with cities struck by Iranian missiles.
incidents.impact: Campaign designed to support kinetic operations and bombing damage assessment. Targeted municipal governments handling emergency response to missile strikes. Activity ongoing in three waves across March.

incidents.victim: Amazon Web Services Bahrain datacenter
incidents.vector: IRGC-affiliated forces conducted kinetic missile/drone strike on Amazon's cloud computing facility in Bahrain as part of broader escalation targeting civilian and industrial infrastructure in the Gulf.
incidents.impact: AWS Bahrain taken offline. IRGC-affiliated media announced further waves of strikes targeting steel industries in Abu Dhabi and Bahrain, and threatened major bridges across Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, UAE, and Jordan.

incidents.victim: Organizations using Medusa ransomware targets (healthcare, education, professional services, finance — US, UK, Australia)
incidents.vector: Storm-1175 exploits N-day and zero-day vulnerabilities in web-facing systems (SmarterMail CVE-2026-23760, GoAnywhere CVE-2025-10035) to deploy Medusa ransomware, often within 24 hours of initial access.
incidents.impact: Recent victims include University of Mississippi Medical Center (fully reopened Mar 2) and Passaic County, NJ. North Korean Lazarus group also observed deploying Medusa ransomware.

incidents.victim: Organizations using FortiClient EMS (nearly 2,000 publicly exposed instances)
incidents.vector: Active exploitation of zero-day CVE-2026-35616 (CVSS 9.8) in FortiClient EMS allowing unauthenticated remote code execution. Exploitation began March 31, ramping up after Fortinet issued a hotfix on April 5.
incidents.impact: Second FortiClient EMS vulnerability exploited in three weeks. CISA added to KEV catalog. Full patch still pending; only hotfix available. Attackers deliberately timed exploitation over Easter weekend.

incidents.victim: Organizations using Citrix NetScaler ADC and Gateway
incidents.vector: Exploitation of CVE-2026-3055 (CVSS 9.3) in Citrix NetScaler ADC allowing unauthenticated attackers to leak sensitive memory from deployments, similar to previous CitrixBleed vulnerabilities.
incidents.impact: CISA ordered federal agencies to patch by Thursday. Vulnerability has hallmarks of CitrixBleed and Citrix Bleed Two, which previously enabled ransomware gangs and nation-state hackers to attack government organizations and major companies.

incidents.victim: Southeast Asian government entities (dozens of agencies via TrueConf server)
incidents.vector: Exploited CVE-2026-3502 in TrueConf video conferencing software to distribute weaponized updates via a compromised government-operated on-premises server, deploying Havoc penetration tool and ShadowPad malware.
incidents.impact: Dozens of government entities across the country received malicious updates through their trusted video conferencing platform. CISA gave federal agencies until April 16 to patch.

incidents.victim: Iran-based organizations and infrastructure
incidents.vector: TeamPCP deployed CanisterWorm variant with a wiper payload that detects Iranian timezone and Farsi language settings, then destroys data on local machines and Kubernetes clusters.
incidents.impact: If the wiper detects access to a Kubernetes cluster in an Iranian environment, it destroys data on every node. Otherwise it wipes the local machine. Represents a cybercriminal group injecting itself into the Iran conflict.

incidents.victim: Russia providing cyber support to Iran
incidents.vector: Russian satellites conducted at least 24 imagery surveys of military facilities across 11 Middle Eastern countries covering 46 targets; Russian and Iranian hackers collaborated in the cyber domain.
incidents.impact: Ukrainian intelligence assessment found military bases and headquarters were targeted by Iranian strikes within days of being surveyed by Russian satellites. Represents most detailed account of Russian secret support to Iran.

incidents.victim: Organizations using F5 BIG-IP Access Policy Manager
incidents.vector: Active exploitation of CVE-2025-53521, an unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability in F5 BIG-IP APM, a common component in large enterprises.
incidents.impact: F5 confirmed active exploitation. NCSC urged UK organizations to take immediate action, recommending isolation of affected systems and investigation for compromise. Indicators of compromise published.

incidents.victim: Organizations worldwide via WhatsApp
incidents.vector: WhatsApp messages deliver malicious VBScript files that initiate a multi-stage infection chain using renamed Windows utilities, retrieving payloads from AWS, Tencent Cloud, and Backblaze B2, and installing MSI backdoors.
incidents.impact: Campaign combines social engineering with living-off-the-land techniques, using cloud-hosted payloads and legitimate tools to establish persistence and remote access while evading detection.

incidents.victim: High-risk individuals (government officials, diplomats)
incidents.vector: Russian-based actors targeting messaging apps (WhatsApp, Signal, Messenger) to trick high-risk individuals into sharing login codes, adding attacker devices to accounts, or clicking phishing links and QR codes.
incidents.impact: NCSC and international partners issued joint advisory warning of growing malicious activity. Attackers impersonate known contacts to access sensitive information and join group chats undetected.

incidents.victim: Hundreds of organizations (manufacturing, education, IT, healthcare, finance, agriculture)
incidents.vector: Akira ransomware group uses zero-day vulnerabilities, purchased exploits, and VPNs lacking MFA for initial access, employing intermittent encryption to move from breach to full encryption in under one hour.
incidents.impact: At least $245 million in ransom payments collected through September 2025. Group offers functional decryptors to incentivize payment. FBI and CISA flagged Akira as a top global ransomware group.

incidents.victim: Jonathan Spalletta charged (Uranium Finance crypto exchange — $53M stolen)
incidents.vector: Exploited smart contract vulnerabilities in the Uranium Finance decentralized cryptocurrency exchange across 26 liquidity pools, then laundered proceeds through Tornado Cash mixer.
incidents.impact: DOJ unsealed indictment; $31M in cryptocurrency seized. Defendant spent stolen funds on a $500K Black Lotus Magic card, $1.5M in sealed Alpha Booster packs, rare Pokemon cards, and an antique Roman coin commemorating Caesar's assassination.

incidents.victim: Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid, Mossad botnets dismantled (3M+ compromised IoT devices)
incidents.vector: Four botnets comprising 3 million+ compromised IoT devices (routers, web cameras) used for record-setting DDoS attacks, including against Department of Defense infrastructure.
incidents.impact: DOJ and authorities in Canada and Germany seized domains and infrastructure. Botnets launched hundreds of thousands of DDoS attacks with extortion demands against victims.
